From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Formula car racing class

This article is about the second-tier of single-seater racing. For the current international championship of the same name, see [FIA Formula 2 Championship](/wiki/FIA_Formula_2_Championship "FIA Formula 2 Championship").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/RindtJochen1970LotusF2.jpg/250px-RindtJochen1970LotusF2.jpg)](/wiki/File:RindtJochen1970LotusF2.jpg)

[Jochen Rindt](/wiki/Jochen_Rindt "Jochen Rindt") driving a Formula 2 Lotus in 1970 at the [Nürburgring](/wiki/N%C3%BCrburgring "Nürburgring")

**Formula Two** (**F2**) is a type of [open-wheel](/wiki/Open-wheel_car "Open-wheel car") [formula racing](/wiki/Formula_racing "Formula racing") category first codified in 1948. It was replaced in 1985 by [Formula 3000](/wiki/Formula_3000 "Formula 3000"), but revived by the [FIA](/wiki/FIA "FIA") from [2009](/wiki/2009_FIA_Formula_Two_Championship_season "2009 FIA Formula Two Championship season") to [2012](/wiki/2012_FIA_Formula_Two_Championship_season "2012 FIA Formula Two Championship season") in the form of the [FIA Formula Two Championship](/wiki/FIA_Formula_Two_Championship_(2009%E2%80%932012) "FIA Formula Two Championship (2009–2012)"). The name returned again in 2017 when the former [GP2 Series](/wiki/GP2_Series "GP2 Series") became known as the [FIA Formula 2 Championship](/wiki/FIA_Formula_2_Championship "FIA Formula 2 Championship").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

While [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") has generally been regarded as the pinnacle of open-wheeled auto racing, the high-performance nature of the cars and the expense involved in the series has always meant a need for a path to reach this peak. For much of the history of Formula One, Formula Two has represented the penultimate step on the motorsport ladder.

### Pre-war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pre-war")]

Prior to the Second World War, there usually existed a division of racing for cars smaller and less powerful than Grand Prix racers. This category was usually called [voiturette](/wiki/Voiturette "Voiturette") ("small car") racing and provided a means for amateur or less experienced drivers and smaller marques to prove themselves. By the outbreak of war, the rules for voiturette racing permitted 1.5 L supercharged engines; Grand Prix cars were permitted 3.0 L supercharged or 4.5 L [naturally aspirated](/wiki/Naturally_aspirated_engine "Naturally aspirated engine").

### Official beginnings (post war–1953)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Official beginnings (post war–1953)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Ferrari_166_F2_-_Museo_Ferrari_%2817946190090%29.jpg/250px-Ferrari_166_F2_-_Museo_Ferrari_%2817946190090%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ferrari_166_F2_-_Museo_Ferrari_(17946190090).jpg)

[Ferrari 166 F2](/wiki/Ferrari_166_F2 "Ferrari 166 F2")

In 1946, the 3.0 L supercharged rules were abandoned and Formulas A and B (later 1 and 2) introduced, effective from 1 January 1947. Formula A permitted the old 4.5 L naturally aspirated cars, but as the 3.0 L supercharged cars were more than a match for these (and the pre-War German and Italian cars were no longer available), the old 1.5 L *voiturette* formula replaced 3.0 L supercharged cars in an attempt to equalise performance.

This left no category below Formula A/Formula One, so Formula Two (originally known as Formula B) was first formally codified by the [FIA](/wiki/FIA "FIA") as a smaller and cheaper complement to the Grand Prix cars of the era to be effective from 1 January 1948 . Among the races held in this first year of Formula Two was the [1948 Stockholm Grand Prix](/wiki/1948_Stockholm_Grand_Prix "1948 Stockholm Grand Prix").

In 1948 [Scuderia Ferrari](/wiki/Scuderia_Ferrari "Scuderia Ferrari") built the [Ferrari 166 F2](/wiki/Ferrari_166_F2 "Ferrari 166 F2"), which made its racing debut at the Florence Grand Prix on September 26, 1948.

The rules limited engines to two-litre naturally aspirated or 500 cc supercharged (an option very rarely used). As a result, the cars were smaller, lighter, and cheaper than those used in Formula One. This encouraged new marques such as [Cooper](/wiki/Cooper_Car_Company "Cooper Car Company") to move up to Formula Two, before competing against large manufacturers like [Alfa Romeo](/wiki/Alfa_Romeo_(Formula_One) "Alfa Romeo (Formula One)") and [Maserati](/wiki/Maserati "Maserati").

In fact, Formula One in its early years attracted so few entrants that in 1952 and 1953 all World Championship [Grand Prix](/wiki/Grand_Prix_motor_racing "Grand Prix motor racing") races, except the unique [Indianapolis 500](/wiki/Indianapolis_500 "Indianapolis 500"), were run in Formula Two (there were, however, non-championship Formula One events).

### 1.5-litre era (1957–1960)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1.5-litre era (1957–1960)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Porsche_718_F1_von_Carel_de_Beaufort%2C_Leidschendam-Museum.jpg/250px-Porsche_718_F1_von_Carel_de_Beaufort%2C_Leidschendam-Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Porsche_718_F1_von_Carel_de_Beaufort,_Leidschendam-Museum.jpg)

1.5-litre Porsche 718

F2 went into decline with the arrival of the 2.5 L F1 in 1954 (with small-capacity sports car racing becoming particularly popular), but a new Formula Two was introduced for 1957, for 1.5 L cars. This became dominated by rear-engined Coopers drawing on their [Formula 3](/wiki/Formula_3 "Formula 3") and "Bobtail" sports car, with [Porsches](/wiki/Porsche "Porsche") based on their RSK sports cars enjoying some success. [Ferrari](/wiki/Ferrari "Ferrari") originally developed their "Sharknose" [Dino 156](/wiki/Dino_(automobile)#Dino_156_F2 "Dino (automobile)") as a Formula Two car, while still racing front-engined Grand Prix cars. The dominant engine of this formula was the [Coventry Climax FPF](/wiki/Coventry-Climax#FPF "Coventry-Climax") four-cylinder, with the rare [Borgward](/wiki/Borgward "Borgward") sixteen-valve unit enjoying some success.

A slightly enlarged version of the F2 [Cooper](/wiki/Cooper_Car_Company "Cooper Car Company") won the first two Formula One Grands Prix in 1958, marking the beginning of the rear-engined era in Formula One. The 1.5 L formula was short-lived, with [Formula Junior](/wiki/Formula_Junior "Formula Junior") effectively replacing first Formula Three and then Formula Two until 1963—but the 1961 1.5 L Formula One was effectively a continuation of this Formula Two. For 1960, in preparation for the new Formula One, two international championships were held, the [Formula Two Constructors' Championship and the Formula Two Drivers' Championship](/wiki/1960_Formula_Two_season "1960 Formula Two season"). The Drivers' Championship was won by [Jack Brabham](/wiki/Jack_Brabham "Jack Brabham"), while the Manufacturers' Championship ended in a tie between Porsche and Cooper.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

### Formula Junior (1961–1963) and 1-litre Formula Two (1964–1966)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Formula Junior (1961–1963) and 1-litre Formula Two (1964–1966)")]

[Formula Junior](/wiki/Formula_Junior "Formula Junior") was introduced in 1959, an attempt to be all things to all people (both a training formula replacing Formula Three and a high-level international category below Formula One replacing Formula Two), and it was soon realised that there was a need to split it into two new formulae; Formula Two and Formula Three were reintroduced for the 1964 season, with Formula Three requiring one-litre production-based engines, which were similar to Formula Junior with very restricted tuning, and Formula Two also having a 1.0 L engine size, but permitting pure-bred racing engines. Formula Two was largely the domain of Formula One stars on their days off. Engines were mostly by [Cosworth](/wiki/Cosworth "Cosworth") (based on Ford blocks) and [Honda](/wiki/Honda "Honda"), though some other units appeared, including various [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat") based units and dedicated racing engines from [BMC](/wiki/British_Motor_Corporation "British Motor Corporation") and [BRM](/wiki/British_Racing_Motors "British Racing Motors").

### 1.6-litre era and driver grading (1967–1971)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1.6-litre era and driver grading (1967–1971)")]

For 1967, the [FIA](/wiki/FIA "FIA") increased the maximum engine capacity to 1600cc.[[2]](#cite_note-2) With the "return to power" of Formula One the gap between Formula One and Formula Two was felt to be too wide, and the introduction of new 1600cc production-based engine regulations for Formula Two restored the category to its intended role as a feeder series for Formula One. The [FIA](/wiki/F%C3%A9d%C3%A9ration_Internationale_de_l%27Automobile "Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile") also introduced the [European Formula Two Championship](/wiki/European_Formula_Two_Championship "European Formula Two Championship") in 1967. Ickx, driving a [Matra](/wiki/Equipe_Matra_Sports "Equipe Matra Sports") MS5, won the inaugural championship by 11 points from the Australian, [Frank Gardner](/wiki/Frank_Gardner_(driver) "Frank Gardner (driver)").

The most popular 1600cc engine was the [Cosworth](/wiki/Cosworth "Cosworth") FVA, the sixteen-valve head on a four-cylinder [Cortina](/wiki/Ford_Cortina "Ford Cortina") block that was effectively the "proof of concept" for the legendary DFV. The 1967 FVA gave 220 bhp (160 kW; 220 PS) at 9000 rpm. Other units also appeared, including a four-cylinder [BMW](/wiki/BMW "BMW") and a V6 [Dino](/wiki/Ferrari_Dino_engine "Ferrari Dino engine") [Ferrari](/wiki/Ferrari "Ferrari").

Nevertheless, many Formula One drivers continued to drive the smaller and lighter cars on non-championship weekends, and some Grand Prix grids (notably in Germany, where the long circuit at the [Nürburgring](/wiki/N%C3%BCrburgring "Nürburgring") could cope with large entries) would be a mix of Formula One and Formula Two cars. [Jacky Ickx](/wiki/Jacky_Ickx "Jacky Ickx") made his Grand Prix debut at the Nürburgring in a Formula Two car in 1966. The following year Ickx qualified with the third fastest time overall but was forced to start behind the slower Formula One cars. Ickx quickly forced his way back into a points position, only to be forced to retire with broken suspension. [Jim Clark](/wiki/Jim_Clark "Jim Clark"), regarded as one of the greatest race drivers of all time, was killed in a Formula Two race early in 1968, at the [Hockenheimring](/wiki/Hockenheimring "Hockenheimring"). A year later [Gerhard Mitter](/wiki/Gerhard_Mitter "Gerhard Mitter") was killed at the Nürburgring while practising for the [1969 German Grand Prix](/wiki/1969_German_Grand_Prix "1969 German Grand Prix") in a Formula Two car.

The "invasion" of Formula One drivers in Formula Two ranks (a situation similar to that of [buschwhacking](/wiki/Buschwhacker "Buschwhacker") in modern-day [NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR")) was permitted because of the unique grading system used. Any driver with an *A* grading was not permitted to score championship points. A driver gained an *A* rating via various means which changed somewhat over the years, such as finishing in the points in two Grand Prix events or the top three in two [World Sports Car](/wiki/World_Sports_Car "World Sports Car") events. The annual Formula Two champion was also granted an *A* rating for one year, and a Formula One World Champion was *A* graded for five. This system permitted less experienced drivers to work towards the championship and forward their careers, while allowing senior drivers to keep their hand in during the long breaks between Grands Prix of the time.

In the early years of the 1600cc formula, Brabham and Lotus were the most numerous constructors, although Ferrari intermittently entered a works team, as did BMW (with [Lola](/wiki/Lola_Cars "Lola Cars") and [Dornier](/wiki/Dornier_Flugzeugwerke "Dornier Flugzeugwerke")-built chassis). A number of smaller constructors such as Matra and Tecno were successful. [Chevron](/wiki/Chevron_Cars_Ltd "Chevron Cars Ltd") also provided cars. The French firm Matra won the three first editions of the European championship, with Tecno winning the fourth.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Peter_scharmann_formel2_1978.jpg/250px-Peter_scharmann_formel2_1978.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peter_scharmann_formel2_1978.jpg)

[Peter Scharmann](/wiki/Peter_Scharmann "Peter Scharmann")'s 1978 [March](/wiki/March_Engineering "March Engineering")–[BMW](/wiki/BMW "BMW")

### 2.0-litre era (1972–1984)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2.0-litre era (1972–1984)")]

In 1972, the formula was changed to increase power by permitting 2.0 L production-based engines—Cosworth BDs and BMW four-cylinder engines dominated the early years, with BMW-powered Marches gradually establishing dominance. For 1976, engines developed purely for racing were permitted to compete, with [Renault](/wiki/Renault "Renault") developing a particularly potent V6; allied to a sponsorship scheme from oil company Elf the formula was briefly dominated by French teams and drivers; BMW started to back a works March team and raised the stakes in the late-Seventies. Even the [Ferrari](/wiki/Ferrari "Ferrari") engine returned briefly with minimal success. The [Hart](/wiki/Hart_(racing) "Hart (racing)") 420R (ultimately derived from the Cosworth BDA) was briefly successful in Marches and Team Surtees won the European F2 Championship in 1972 with Hart engines, driver Mike Hailwood, but most notably in the works [Toleman](/wiki/Toleman "Toleman") team's cars. Dominant chassis of this era were generally from [March](/wiki/March_Engineering "March Engineering") and [Ralt](/wiki/Ralt "Ralt"), with Chevron, the French Elf and [Martinis](/wiki/Martini_(cars) "Martini (cars)") and German [Maurers](/wiki/Maurer_Motorsport_(Germany) "Maurer Motorsport (Germany)") being briefly successful.

[Honda](/wiki/Honda "Honda") returned to Formula Two in the early 1980s with a powerful V6, but this was very much a *works* engine and the cost of competing with the works Ralt-Honda team became prohibitive. As a result, grids diminished in size and declining interest in the series threatened it with extinction.

### Superseded

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Superseded")]

After the 1984 season, the FIA replaced Formula Two with the newly created [Formula 3000](/wiki/Formula_3000 "Formula 3000") category, which was designed to cut the cost of competition. The initial regulations joined F2-style chassis with the naturally aspirated 3000 cc [Cosworth DFV](/wiki/Cosworth_DFV "Cosworth DFV") V8 engines, that were by then obsolete in the all-[turbocharged](/wiki/Turbocharged "Turbocharged") Formula One. The premier series was the [FIA Formula 3000 International Championship](/wiki/International_Formula_3000 "International Formula 3000"), though a number of other championships were also run to Formula 3000 regulations.

### Revival (2009–2012)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Revival (2009–2012)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Dean_stoneman.jpg/250px-Dean_stoneman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dean_stoneman.jpg)

[Dean Stoneman](/wiki/Dean_Stoneman "Dean Stoneman") won the championship in 2010.

Main article: [FIA Formula Two Championship (2009–2012)](/wiki/FIA_Formula_Two_Championship_(2009%E2%80%932012) "FIA Formula Two Championship (2009–2012)")

Following an absence of 25 years, a Formula Two championship was re-established by the FIA in 2009.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Unlike the previous incarnation, the [FIA Formula Two Championship](/wiki/FIA_Formula_Two_Championship_(2009%E2%80%932012) "FIA Formula Two Championship (2009–2012)") was a [one make](/wiki/One_make_racing "One make racing") series, with the competitors all using the same chassis and engine. [Jonathan Palmer](/wiki/Jonathan_Palmer "Jonathan Palmer")'s [MotorSport Vision](/wiki/MotorSport_Vision "MotorSport Vision") company managed the championship and also operated and maintained the cars on behalf of the drivers.[[4]](#cite_note-4) There were no teams in the championship – unlike other professional motorsport series each driver instead worked with engineers supplied by MotorSport Vision. The cars were designed by [Williams Grand Prix Engineering](/wiki/Williams_Grand_Prix_Engineering "Williams Grand Prix Engineering"), and were powered by a 1.8L turbocharged Audi engine developed by [Mountune Racing](/wiki/Mountune_Racing "Mountune Racing"), with 480 brake horsepower (358 kW; 487 PS). The cars fell between Formula One and Formula Three in performance, and cost each driver £195,000 for a season.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The 2009 championship comprised eight events in Europe between the months of May and November. There were two races per weekend – each 110 km long (around 40 minutes). Two 30-minute practice sessions and two 30-minute qualifying sessions preceded both races.[[6]](#cite_note-grandprix.com-6) The inaugural championship was won by Andy Soucek although was marred by the death of [Henry Surtees](/wiki/Henry_Surtees "Henry Surtees") during the fourth round at Brands Hatch.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The series was discontinued by the FIA and MSV after the 2012 season.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The championship had struggled to attract enough drivers in the final year, with the single team concept proving to be unpopular compared to championships run on a more conventional basis.[[9]](#cite_note-F2_cancelled-9) Despite being marketed as a feeder series for Formula One, the competing [GP2](/wiki/GP2_Series "GP2 Series") and [Formula Renault 3.5](/wiki/Formula_Renault_3.5 "Formula Renault 3.5") series had significantly more powerful cars,[[9]](#cite_note-F2_cancelled-9) and no Formula Two driver had managed to progress to a Formula One team until [Jolyon Palmer](/wiki/Jolyon_Palmer "Jolyon Palmer") in [2016](/wiki/2016_Formula_One_season "2016 Formula One season") with [Renault Sport F1 Team](/wiki/Renault_in_Formula_One "Renault in Formula One").

### Second revival (2017)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Second revival (2017)")]

Main article: [FIA Formula 2 Championship](/wiki/FIA_Formula_2_Championship "FIA Formula 2 Championship")

In 2015, the FIA announced plans to overhaul the [Super Licence](/wiki/FIA_Super_Licence "FIA Super Licence") system, streamlining the qualification criteria and weighting the various feeder series to allow drivers a more linear progress system. Included with this were plans for a revival of the Formula Two category, which was given priority over all other categories of competition. Further details were published by the World Motorsport Council, revealing plans to model the new Formula Two championship along the same lines as the revived [European Formula 3 Championship](/wiki/FIA_European_Formula_3_Championship "FIA European Formula 3 Championship") and the newly introduced [Formula 4](/wiki/FIA_Formula_4 "FIA Formula 4") category.[[10]](#cite_note-3rd_time_lucky-10) The series will be a part of the [FIA Global Pathway](/wiki/FIA_Global_Pathway "FIA Global Pathway").

Rather than reviving the series by creating a new one where none had previously existed, the FIA chose to rebrand the [GP2 Series](/wiki/GP2_Series "GP2 Series") as the *FIA Formula 2 Championship* in early 2017. However, like the series that lasted from 2009 to 2012, there has been a fatality, that being Frenchman [Anthoine Hubert](/wiki/Anthoine_Hubert "Anthoine Hubert") who was killed at the [2019 Spa-Francorchamps round](/wiki/2019_Spa-Francorchamps_Formula_2_round "2019 Spa-Francorchamps Formula 2 round") after being struck by [Juan Manuel Correa](/wiki/Juan_Manuel_Correa "Juan Manuel Correa") at a near-90° angle. Since the inception of FIA Formula 2, drivers to have graduated to F1 include [Charles Leclerc](/wiki/Charles_Leclerc "Charles Leclerc"), [George Russell](/wiki/George_Russell_(racing_driver) "George Russell (racing driver)"), [Oscar Piastri](/wiki/Oscar_Piastri "Oscar Piastri") and [Gabriel Bortoleto](/wiki/Gabriel_Bortoleto "Gabriel Bortoleto"). All eleven teams run the [Dallara F2 2024](/wiki/Dallara_F2_2024 "Dallara F2 2024") chassis, powered by a [Mecachrome](/wiki/Mecachrome "Mecachrome") engine.

## Other Formula Two series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Other Formula Two series")]

### Soviet Formula 2 Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Soviet Formula 2 Championship")]

Main article: [Soviet Formula 2 Championship](/wiki/Soviet_Formula_2_Championship "Soviet Formula 2 Championship")

The Central Automotoclub of the USSR (CAMK) joined the FIA in October 1956, aiming to develop Soviet motorsport after seeing international races like the British F1 GP. In 1959, CAMK introduced a new classification system for racing cars in the USSR. However, F2 cars were not part of the 1960 Soviet motorsport championship because there were no suitable stock engines available, and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") did not produce specialized racing engines at that time. Consequently, it wasn't until 1962 that the F2 category made its debut in Soviet autosport championships. Two years later, updated F2 regulations were introduced. It wasn't until 1971 that F2 cars started to regularly appear on Soviet tracks, and F2 series were included in Soviet motorsport championships until 1977.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Japanese Formula Two

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Japanese Formula Two")]

Main article: [Super Formula](/wiki/Super_Formula "Super Formula")

Prior to the Formula Nippon, during the 1.6 L 1967–1971 era, a Formula 2 series championship existed in Japan. Japanese teams were building their own cars and engines. As for races ran in Europe, the series was simply called Formula 2. Amongst the makes, the best known was the Mitsubishi Colt. Cars raced with parallel FIA rules, using 1600 cc displacement engines, from 1967 to 1970.

Japan ran a series known as "Japanese Formula 2000 championship" to rules similar to two-litre F2 between 1973 and 1977. Production-based single-cam engines were permitted to run at 2.4 L, but they soon came into line with FIA rules in the mid-1970s. The Japanese Formula 2 championship ran between 1978 and 1986, before Japan too adopted a set of regulations very similar to F3000 rules, with the series being known as Japanese Formula 3000 from 1987. The series is now known as [Super Formula](/wiki/Super_Formula "Super Formula"), after using the name Formula Nippon from 1996 to 2012. Japan also ran a series for full-bodied versions of F2 cars called *[Fuji Grand Champion Series](/wiki/Fuji_Grand_Champion_Series "Fuji Grand Champion Series")*—somewhat akin to the F5000-based revival of [Can-Am](/wiki/Can-Am "Can-Am").

### Australian Formula 2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Australian Formula 2")]

Main article: [Australian Formula 2](/wiki/Australian_Formula_2 "Australian Formula 2")

Australia has had its own Australian Formula 2 category since 1964.[[12]](#cite_note-12) From 1978, the formula has specified that cars be powered by a production-based single-cam engine with a capacity between 1100 cc and 1600 cc.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Induction must be by means of carburettor/s, the engine must be naturally aspirated and an 8500 rpm rev limit is applied.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

An Australian Formula 2 Championship was sanctioned by the [Confederation of Australian Motor Sport](/wiki/Confederation_of_Australian_Motor_Sport "Confederation of Australian Motor Sport") in the years from 1964 to 1965, from 1969 to 1977 and from 1979 to 1988.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Mexican Formula Two

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Mexican Formula Two")]

[Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") ran its own Formula Two series (previously known as Formula K) for 12 years in the early 1990s. The cars were similar to [Formula Atlantic](/wiki/Formula_Atlantic "Formula Atlantic") vehicles, fitted with 2.2 L [Chrysler](/wiki/Chrysler "Chrysler") engines.

### British Formula Two

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: British Formula Two")]

See also: [British Formula 3000](/wiki/British_Formula_3000 "British Formula 3000")

Formula Two racing was popular in the United Kingdom in the late 1940s and early 1950s. During the 2.0 litre engine era, [HWM](/wiki/Hersham_%26_Walton_Motors "Hersham & Walton Motors"), [Connaught](/wiki/Connaught_Engineering "Connaught Engineering") and [Cooper](/wiki/Cooper_Car_Company "Cooper Car Company") were among the constructors who built cars for the class, usually powered by [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol_(automobile) "Bristol (automobile)"). When F2 was brought back in 1.5 litre form in 1956, Cooper and [Lotus](/wiki/Team_Lotus "Team Lotus") were the first to build cars to the new specification, taking advantage of the availability of [Coventry Climax](/wiki/Coventry_Climax "Coventry Climax") engines.

Championships were held from 1956 to 1960, 1964 to 1967 and again in 1972.[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Stirling Moss](/wiki/Stirling_Moss "Stirling Moss"),[[17]](#cite_note-17) [Jack Brabham](/wiki/Jack_Brabham "Jack Brabham"), [Jim Clark](/wiki/Jim_Clark "Jim Clark") and [Niki Lauda](/wiki/Niki_Lauda "Niki Lauda") won titles.

The British Formula 3000 series was briefly known as "Formula Two" in an attempt to make its status more apparent to casual spectators. However, the series was in terminal decline and the change of name did not help. At one point the use of 4.2 L TVR engines cars in F3000 chassis was discussed as a possible British F2, but this did not materialise.

### Trophées de France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Formula_Two&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Trophées de France")]

See also: [Trophées de France](/wiki/Troph%C3%A9es_de_France "Trophées de France")

Prior to the formation of the European Championship, France held its own, very popular championship between 1964 and 1967. Despite only running for four seasons, the [Brabham](/wiki/Brabham "Brabham") marque won three titles, with the drivers going to Jack Brabham (twice), Jim Clark and [Jochen Rindt](/wiki/Jochen_Rindt "Jochen Rindt"). Races were held at some of the most legendary venues in France, [Pau](/wiki/Pau_Grand_Prix "Pau Grand Prix"), [Clermont-Ferrand](/wiki/Charade_Circuit "Charade Circuit") and [Reims](/wiki/Reims-Gueux "Reims-Gueux"), to name just three.

However once the European Championship had successfully completed this first season, the French version was abandoned.
